attack_narrative: A threat actor with access to the shieldbase.lan domain leveraged WMI to remotely execute PowerShell on BASE-WKSTN-05 (172.16.7.15) beginning 2018-08-31 01:14:44 UTC. The attacker deployed a Cobalt Strike SMB Beacon via a three-stage process: (1) WmiPrvSE.exe spawned a 64-bit PowerShell session, (2) the 64-bit PS decoded and decompressed a gzip-wrapped shellcode payload from a base64-encoded command, (3) on detecting a 64-bit OS, it re-launched as a 32-bit process via start-job -RunAs32 to execute the x86 Cobalt Strike reflective DLL loader. This pattern was repeated three times between 01:14 and 01:31 UTC. The beacon communicated via SMB named pipe \\.\ pipe\diagsvc-22, indicating this workstation was part of a lateral movement chain — not the initial point of compromise. Post-exploitation included 5 rundll32.exe injections from PID 1332 between 01:31 and 21:07 on Aug 31. An orphaned rundll32.exe (PID 7100) persisted until memory capture on Sep 6. The attack succeeded despite active McAfee AV and Sysmon with Cobalt Strike detection rules.

kill_chain.detail: Not directly observed on this host. WMI execution implies the attacker already had domain credentials and network access from a previously compromised system.
WMI remote execution (WmiPrvSE.exe → powershell.exe) delivered encoded Cobalt Strike stager. Three execution chains created between 01:14-01:31 UTC.
Encoded PowerShell (-nop -w hidden -encodedcommand), Wow64 process re-launch, reflective DLL injection via VirtualAlloc, rundll32.exe as injection host. Evaded McAfee AV and Sysmon.
kill_chain.mitre: T1027 Obfuscated Files, T1055.001 DLL Injection, T1218.011 Rundll32

kill_chain.detail: Cobalt Strike SMB Beacon over named pipe \\.\ pipe\diagsvc-22. Lateral movement C2 channel — not direct external comms from this host.
kill_chain.mitre: T1071.002 SMB, T1572 Protocol Tunneling (named pipe)

kill_chain.detail: This workstation is a lateral movement target, accessed via WMI from another domain-joined system. The SMB Beacon pipe suggests it may also serve as a pivot to additional hosts.
kill_chain.mitre: T1021.006 WMI, T1570 Lateral Tool Transfer

kill_chain.detail: Multiple long-running PowerShell processes in Session 0. Orphaned rundll32.exe (PID 7100) survived for 6+ days. No registry or service-based persistence observed in memory (may exist on disk).

findings.description: Cobalt Strike SMB Beacon deployed via WMI lateral movement with Wow64 PowerShell injection cradle. Named pipe C2: diagsvc-22.
Post-exploitation via rundll32 injection. PID 1332 spawned 5 rundll32.exe instances for additional code execution.
Orphaned rundll32.exe (PID 7100) persisted 6+ days with missing parent — likely long-running beacon.
Attack evaded active McAfee AV and Sysmon. Reflective injection and in-memory execution bypassed signature detection.
citations: [INFERENCE: AV processes running per psscan, Sysmon config with CS rules per strings, attack still successful]

findings.description: ACH analysis: Targeted APT intrusion is most plausible (0 inconsistencies). Commodity malware, insider threat, and legitimate activity eliminated.
citations: [INFERENCE: ACH — H1(0 inconsistencies) vs H2(3), H3(4), H4(6), H5(1)]

competing_hypotheses.runner_up: H5 — Red team (eliminated by scenario context, not forensic evidence)

unresolved_questions: What was the initial access vector? (Requires disk/log analysis from other hosts)
Which host executed the WMI command against base-wkstn-05? (Requires DC event logs)
What specific actions did the attacker take via rundll32 injections? (Requires disk forensics or Sysmon logs)
Was data exfiltrated from this workstation? (Memory alone cannot confirm)
Are other workstations in the shieldbase.lan domain also compromised? (Requires network-wide analysis)
What credentials did the attacker use? (LSASS dump or DC logs needed)
